text: The surgeon uses a suture with a large straight needle at each end to retract the round ligament, passing the needles through the abdominal wall and securing them outside the patient. This innovative liver retraction method frees the third robotic arm for other tasks during the procedure.

The photograph shows a suture passed through the skin and secured by a surgical instrument. The hepatic flexure of the colon is widely mobilized, and a cloture maneuver is performed using robotic surgery. Care is taken to avoid perforating the duodenal wall with excessive traction from the robotic arm. After completing the cloture maneuver and removing some adhesions, the first jejunal loop is retracted through the mesenteric opening created by duodenal mobilization and transection of the ligament of Treitz, all within the supracolic compartment.

The surgeon transects the first jejunal loop using an endoscopic stapler. The superior pancreaticoduodenal artery is dissected and divided between polymer locking systems, following verification of the common hepatic artery pulse.

The surgeon dissects the main bile duct within the hepatoduodenal ligament, transects it, and removes the visible biliary stent. The distal segment of the duct is closed with a locking system, while a detachable laparoscopic bulldog clamp is applied to the proximal segment to prevent leakage during the procedure. The sectioning of the main bile duct is completed.

The surgeon transects the first part of the duodenum using a stapler after ligating and transecting the gastroepiploic vessels and pyloric artery. The inferior pancreatic border is dissected to locate the mesenterico-portal confluence. A tunnel is developed around the superior mesenteric vein and portal confluence, with an instrument passed in front of the portal confluence and under the pancreatic body. Vascular tape is used to encircle and retract the pancreas, facilitating the dissection of the pancreatic head and uncinate process from the mesenteric root and vein.

The surgeon carefully dissects the uncinate process and tumor from the mesenteric vein, maintaining a close plane of dissection near the superior mesenteric artery. The dissection is challenging due to inflammation in the pancreatic head and uncinate process, although the tumor does not involve the vein.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to transect the pancreas, carefully identifying and transecting the main pancreatic duct. The pancreatic tissue transection is completed.

The surgeon ligates and transects the inferior pancreatic artery, a branch of the superior mesenteric artery. The dissection of the uncinate process from the mesenteric and superior mesenteric artery continues, progressing until the pancreas and the entire surgical specimen are completely released from the portal vein.

At the end of the resection phase, the surgical field reveals the inferior vena cava, the clamped bile duct, the pancreas, the pancreatic duct, the superior mesenteric vein, portal vein, and the superior mesenteric artery. The reconstruction phase begins with the pancreaticojejunostomy anastomosis.

The first jejunal loop is positioned through the mesenteric defect created during the resection phase. A proline suture on the inferior border of the pancreas is used to stabilize the pancreas with the robotic arm III. The cut surface of the pancreatic remnant is mobilized for approximately one centimeter from the splenic vein to prepare for the anastomosis.

The surgeon performs an end-to-side duct-to-mucosa anastomosis between the pancreatic duct and the jejunum. The posterior outer layer is sutured using 4-0 Prolene on the posterior aspect of the pancreas and the seromuscular layer of the jejunum at the anti-mesenteric border. The jejunum is opened on the anti-mesenteric side corresponding to the pancreatic duct location using a harmonic scalpel, facilitating the anastomosis. The duct-to-mucosa anastomosis is completed with interrupted sutures using Vicryl 5-0.

The robotic system is utilized to perform delicate suturing during the anastomosis, with the surgeon applying sutures using the non-dominant hand. The stable camera and wrist-like movements of the robotic arms enhance precision in the operative field, where an assistant is also present.

The surgeon passes sutures at the three, five, eight, and eleven o'clock positions, along with two anterior sutures, on the anterior aspect of the pancreatic parenchyma. The anterior outer layer is constructed using interrupted Forzero's ProGlean sutures, connecting the pancreatic tissue to the jejunal seromuscular layer.

The surgeon completes the pancreatic jejunal anastomosis and proceeds to the biliodigestive anastomosis. The bulldog clamp is removed from the main bile duct, and an incision is made on the anti-mesenteric border of the jejunum, approximately twenty centimeters from the previous anastomosis site.

The surgeon performs the bileodigestive anastomosis using the robotic surgical system, employing two running sutures, one on the posterior and one on the anterior aspect, with a five-zero Vicryl suture in a single-loop manner.

The liver is retracted using a transcutaneous suture applied to the round ligament, while the third robotic arm is utilized to expose and stabilize the bile duct in a favorable position for anastomosis. The bileodigestive anastomosis is then completed.

The final aspect of the baleodigestive anastomosis is shown, with the jejunal loop used for the terrenes mesocolic anastomosis passing into the supranesocolic compartment. The loop is fixed to the mesocolon to prevent herniation of the jejunum through the mesentery.

The surgeon identifies the jejunal loop emerging from the mesocolon opening through the Trots ligament within the inframesocolic compartment. The loop is positioned to perform a pre-colic duodenum-jejunum anastomosis in an end-to-side fashion. The posterior outer layer is sutured using a PDS four-zero running suture between the seromuscular layers of the duodenum and the jejunal anti-mesenteric border.

The surgeon opens the posterior layer of the duodenum near the stapled tissue and creates an opening on the anti-mesenteric border of the jejunum using a harmonic scalpel. The mucosa is fixed to the serosa of the jejunum with an inner-posterior continuous absorbable suture using PDS four zeros, ensuring all layers are secured. The transection of the stapled portion of the duodenum is completed, and robotic arm number three stabilizes the anastomosis. The inner anterior layer of the anastomosis is then performed.

The surgeon completes the duodenal jejunum anastomosis using an anterior outer interrupted suture with PDS four zeros, ensuring all tissue layers of the duodenum and jejunum are securely joined. This marks the completion of the reconstructive phase of the pancreatic duodenectomy.

The surgeon removes the transcutaneous suture used for liver retraction and places a passive open system drain near the pancreatic jejunal anastomosis. The surgical specimen is retrieved inside a plastic bag through an enlarged trocar site below the umbilicus. A 19 French blade drain is positioned near the bileodigestive anastomosis, and the incisions are sutured, marking the completion of the robotic pancreatic duodenectomy.
